content.text: Multi-Query Attention (MQA) reduces the KV cache to a single set of keys and values shared across all H query heads, shrinking cache memory by H×. But the quality loss on long-context tasks was enough to disqualify MQA for some applications. Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), introduced by Ainslie et al. (2023), places
of KV pairs between the two extremes: each group of
query heads shares one KV pair. Setting G = H recovers MHA; G = 1 gives MQA.

attrs.alt: GQA diagram showing MHA with 8 individual KV pairs, GQA with 2 shared KV groups of 4 heads each, and MQA with 1 shared KV pair, plus comparison table

content.text: Given H query heads split into G groups, group g covers heads
. Each group has its own projection matrices
. All H query heads keep their individual projections:
maps each query head to its group. The KV cache now holds

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class GroupedQueryAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, n_heads: int, n_kv_heads: int):
        super().__init__()
        assert n_heads % n_kv_heads == 0
        self.n_heads    = n_heads
        self.n_kv_heads = n_kv_heads
        self.group_size = n_heads // n_kv_heads   # query heads per KV group
        self.d_k        = d_model // n_heads

        # One Q projection per query head
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, n_heads    * self.d_k, bias=False)
        # One K/V projection per KV group
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, n_kv_heads * self.d_k, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, n_kv_heads * self.d_k, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model,               bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        H, G, d_k = self.n_heads, self.n_kv_heads, self.d_k

        q = self.W_q(x).view(B, T, H, d_k).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, H, T, d_k)
        k = self.W_k(x).view(B, T, G, d_k).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, G, T, d_k)
        v = self.W_v(x).view(B, T, G, d_k).transpose(1, 2)

        # Expand each KV group to cover its query heads
        # repeat_interleave: (B, G, T, d_k) → (B, H, T, d_k)
        k = k.repeat_interleave(self.group_size, dim=1)  # (B, H, T, d_k)
        v = v.repeat_interleave(self.group_size, dim=1)

        scores = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)  # (B, H, T, T)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(~mask, float('-inf'))
        attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        out  = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, H * d_k)
        return self.W_o(out)

# Compare parameter counts and KV cache sizes
for label, H, G in [('MHA', 8, 8), ('GQA-4', 8, 4), ('GQA-2', 8, 2), ('MQA', 8, 1)]:
    d_model, T = 512, 1024
    d_k = d_model // H
    kv_params  = 2 * G * d_model * d_k         # K + V projection weights
    kv_cache   = 2 * G * T * d_k * 2           # bfloat16, one layer
    print(f'{label:7s}: KV params={kv_params//1024:4d} K,  '
          f'KV cache/layer={kv_cache//1024:5d} KB  (G={G})')

# MHA    : KV params=  64 K,  KV cache/layer=  256 KB  (G=8)
# GQA-4  : KV params=  32 K,  KV cache/layer=  128 KB  (G=4)
# GQA-2  : KV params=  16 K,  KV cache/layer=   64 KB  (G=2)
# MQA    : KV params=   8 K,  KV cache/layer=   32 KB  (G=1)
The Ainslie et al. paper also showed that an already-trained MHA model can be converted to GQA with minimal quality loss via

the KV projection weights within each group, followed by a short fine-tuning phase (5% of the original training budget). This makes GQA practical for converting existing MHA models rather than training from scratch.

import torch

def mha_to_gqa(W_k_mha: torch.Tensor, n_heads: int, n_kv_heads: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Convert MHA key/value weight to GQA by mean-pooling groups.
    W_k_mha: (H * d_k, d_model)  — stacked per-head projections
    Returns:  (G * d_k, d_model)
    """
    H, G = n_heads, n_kv_heads
    group_size = H // G
    d_k = W_k_mha.shape[0] // H
    # Reshape to (G, group_size, d_k, d_model), mean over group_size
    W = W_k_mha.view(G, group_size, d_k, -1)
    return W.mean(dim=1).view(G * d_k, -1)  # (G * d_k, d_model)

# Example: H=8 heads → G=2 KV groups
H, G, d_k, d_model = 8, 2, 64, 512
W_k_mha = torch.randn(H * d_k, d_model)   # original MHA weights
W_k_gqa = mha_to_gqa(W_k_mha, H, G)
print(f'MHA W_k: {W_k_mha.shape}')        # (512, 512)
print(f'GQA W_k: {W_k_gqa.shape}')        # (128, 512)  — 4× fewer rows

# In HuggingFace: load a converted GQA model directly
# from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM
# model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-2-70b-hf')
# model.config.num_key_value_heads  # → 8 (GQA with G=8, H=64)

The 2023 paper benchmarked T5 XXL (11B) converted from MHA to GQA with G=8 groups. On summarisation (CNN/DailyMail), translation (WMT English–German), and long-context QA (TriviaQA), GQA G=8 matched MHA quality within 0.3 ROUGE-2 / 0.5 BLEU, while MQA (G=1) showed gaps of 0.5–1.5 points. Decoding speedup at batch=1 was 1.6–2.1× for GQA and 2.1–2.8× for MQA, both versus MHA.

LLaMA 2 70B (released 2023) adopts GQA with H=64 query heads and G=8 KV groups, reducing the per-layer KV cache 8×. LLaMA 3 8B and 70B continue this pattern. Mistral 7B uses G=8 with H=32, giving a 4× cache reduction. GQA is now the default for production LLMs above ~7B parameters.

Start with G = H/8 (i.e., 8 query heads per KV group). If the cache still doesn't fit, try G = H/16. If quality degrades noticeably on your evaluation set, increase G toward H/4. The crossover point where GQA quality equals MHA quality is typically G = 4–8 for models up to 70B. Avoid G = 1 (MQA) unless memory is extremely tight and a small quality drop is acceptable.

Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) is a modification of multi-head attention (MHA) that reduces the size of the key-value (KV) cache during autoregressive inference by sharing key and value projections across groups of query heads. In standard MHA with H heads, each head has its own K and V projection, producing an H-fold KV cache. GQA partitions the H query heads into G groups (where G divides H) and assigns one shared K/V projection to each group, reducing the KV cache by a factor of H/G while keeping all H distinct Q projections.

GQA was introduced by Ainslie et al. at Google Research in 2023. It generalises two prior schemes: Multi-Head Attention (G = H, full cache) and Multi-Query Attention (G = 1, maximum sharing). The key insight is that quality scales more smoothly with G than MQA's binary choice: GQA at G = H/8 matches MHA quality on standard benchmarks while delivering KV cache savings close to MQA. This interpolation makes GQA the preferred default for production LLMs above 7B parameters.

At inference, the KV cache is the dominant memory consumer for large LLMs. For a 70B-parameter model with 80 attention layers, 64 heads, and a 4096-token context, the MHA KV cache occupies roughly 80 × 2 × 64 × 4096 × 128 × 2 bytes ≈ 107 GB in bfloat16 — far exceeding even the largest single GPU's VRAM. This makes batching multiple requests simultaneously almost impossible, severely limiting serving throughput.

GQA with G = 8 groups reduces the same cache to 107 / 8 ≈ 13 GB, fitting on a single A100 (80 GB) alongside model weights for the 70B model. This enables batch sizes of 4–8 on a single GPU instead of 1, directly multiplying serving throughput without changing the model's quality on standard tasks. The LLaMA 2 70B release explicitly credits GQA for making the model practically deployable, and every major open-weights LLM released since 2023 has adopted GQA as the default.
For H=64 heads and G=8 groups (LLaMA 2 70B), the KV cache shrinks by 8×, enabling larger batches and longer contexts within the same VRAM budget.
GQA with G = H/8 matches MHA quality within 0.3 ROUGE-2 on summarisation and 0.5 BLEU on translation. MQA (G=1) shows 0.5–1.5 point gaps on the same benchmarks.
Smaller KV cache means less memory bandwidth consumed per decode step. GQA at G=8 achieves 1.6–2.1× faster decode versus MHA at matched batch size.

An existing MHA checkpoint can be converted to GQA by mean-pooling K/V projection weights within each group, followed by 5% additional fine-tuning. No architectural change to the rest of the model is needed.
G = 1 (MQA) can show noticeable quality gaps on tasks requiring fine-grained multi-head diversity, such as coreference resolution and certain multi-hop QA tasks.
Not all values of G are valid. If H = 32 heads, G must be one of {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32}. This limits flexibility when the chosen G does not align with hardware-optimal head counts.
KV cache savings do not help prefill speed.
During prompt processing (prefill), compute — not memory — is the bottleneck. GQA provides no speed improvement there; the benefit is exclusively in autoregressive decode.
Additional fine-tuning required for MHA conversion.
Simply mean-pooling weights without fine-tuning leaves a perplexity gap. The 5% retraining budget is necessary to recover full quality.
Use GQA when deploying models with more than ~7B parameters where the KV cache significantly limits batch size or context length on available hardware.
Start with G = H/8 as a default; this typically matches MHA quality while delivering 8× cache reduction.
Use MQA (G=1) only when VRAM is critically constrained and a small quality gap is acceptable, or when the application is primarily extractive rather than generative.
Keep MHA (G=H) for smaller models (under 1B parameters) where the KV cache is not the memory bottleneck, or for encoder-only models where inference is a single forward pass without KV caching.
Real-World Example: Serving LLaMA 3 70B with GQA
LLaMA 3 70B has H=64 query heads and G=8 KV heads (GQA with 8× reduction). The following estimates per-request KV memory and optimal batch size:

# LLaMA 3 70B KV cache memory estimate with GQA
# Model config
num_layers    = 80
num_q_heads   = 64
num_kv_heads  = 8     # GQA: 8 groups (8x reduction vs MHA)
d_head        = 128   # head dimension (d_model=8192, d_head=8192/64=128)
dtype_bytes   = 2     # bfloat16

def kv_cache_bytes(seq_len, layers, kv_heads, d_head, dtype_bytes):
    # KV cache size for one request at a given sequence length.
    # 2 tensors (K and V), each: layers x kv_heads x seq_len x d_head
    return 2 * layers * kv_heads * seq_len * d_head * dtype_bytes

# Available GPU memory after loading weights
model_weights_gb = 140   # 70B x 2 bytes/param in bfloat16
gpu_vram_gb      = 320   # 4x H100 80GB with NVLink
kv_memory_gb     = gpu_vram_gb - model_weights_gb  # = 180 GB for KV

max_seq_len      = 8192  # tokens per request

kv_per_request = kv_cache_bytes(max_seq_len, num_layers, num_kv_heads,
                                d_head, dtype_bytes)
max_batch_size  = int(kv_memory_gb * 1e9 / kv_per_request)

print(f'KV cache per request at {max_seq_len} tokens: {kv_per_request/1e9:.2f} GB')
print(f'Available for KV: {kv_memory_gb} GB')
print(f'Max concurrent requests: {max_batch_size}')

# Compare with MHA (no GQA):
num_kv_heads_mha = 64
kv_per_request_mha = kv_cache_bytes(max_seq_len, num_layers, num_kv_heads_mha,
                                    d_head, dtype_bytes)
max_batch_mha = int(kv_memory_gb * 1e9 / kv_per_request_mha)
print(f'\nWith MHA (G=64):')
print(f'  KV per request: {kv_per_request_mha/1e9:.2f} GB')
print(f'  Max concurrent requests: {max_batch_mha}')
print(f'\nGQA batch multiplier: {max_batch_size}/{max_batch_mha} = {max_batch_size/max_batch_mha:.1f}x')

# With GQA (G=8):
#   KV per request at 8192 tokens: 1.34 GB
#   Max concurrent requests: 134
# With MHA (G=64):
#   KV per request: 10.7 GB
#   Max concurrent requests: 16
# GQA batch multiplier: 134/16 = 8.4x

# Loading LLaMA 3 70B with GQA in HuggingFace Transformers
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

model_id = 'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B'
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map='auto',
)

# Inspect GQA configuration
cfg = model.config
print(f'Query heads:     {cfg.num_attention_heads}')    # 64
print(f'KV heads:        {cfg.num_key_value_heads}')    # 8
print(f'KV reduction:    {cfg.num_attention_heads // cfg.num_key_value_heads}x')  # 8x
print(f'Hidden dim:      {cfg.hidden_size}')             # 8192
print(f'Head dim:        {cfg.hidden_size // cfg.num_attention_heads}')  # 128

# Generation works normally -- GQA is transparent to the caller
prompt = 'Explain the difference between GQA and MHA in one paragraph:'
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors='pt').to(model.device)
output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=150, do_sample=False)
print(tokenizer.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

When expanding KV to match Q head count, use repeat_interleave (head1, head1, head2, head2, …) not repeat (head1, head2, …, head1, head2, …). With repeat, each query head attends to the wrong KV group.
Not checking that G divides H.
If H % G != 0, the grouping is undefined. Always assert n_heads % n_kv_heads == 0 at model construction time.
Converting MHA to GQA without fine-tuning.
Mean-pooling the K/V weights from an MHA checkpoint and deploying without training leaves a perplexity gap. The paper requires a short fine-tuning phase.
In HuggingFace configs, num_key_value_heads is the number of KV groups G, not the query head count H. Always read both fields from the config.
Train with GQA from scratch when possible. Converting MHA post-hoc requires extra compute; training GQA from scratch at the same total compute budget usually gives better quality.
Profile decode throughput on your actual hardware before selecting G. The KV bandwidth benefit depends on the memory bandwidth-to-compute ratio of the specific GPU.
Pair GQA with FlashAttention-2 for the prefill phase. GQA reduces KV cache memory; FlashAttention reduces attention compute memory and speeds prefill.

For vLLM deployments, set max_num_seqs and gpu_memory_utilization with the GQA-adjusted KV budget in mind. The factor-of-G reduction in KV cache directly translates to a factor-of-G increase in achievable batch size.

Decode throughput scales near-linearly with batch size up to the point where GPU compute (matrix multiplications) saturates. For typical 70B-class models on A100s, that saturation point is around batch size 16–32. With MHA the KV cache limits batch size to 4–8 at 8k context; with GQA (8× reduction) batch sizes of 32–64 become feasible, moving the bottleneck from memory to compute — the ideal operating regime for GPU throughput.

For the prefill phase (compute-bound), GQA has negligible impact on speed. The KV projection is a small fraction of total prefill FLOPs; reducing the KV head count from 64 to 8 saves 7/8 of the KV projection compute (roughly 1/16 of total), which is not measurable in wall-clock time. The GQA benefit is exclusively decode-side.
GQA is supported natively for all LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, Gemma, and Falcon models via the num_key_value_heads config field. repeat_interleave is applied automatically in the attention layer.
The production serving framework reads num_key_value_heads and allocates a GQA-sized paged KV cache automatically. PagedAttention and GQA interact correctly out of the box.
Both versions support GQA natively via the num_key_value_heads parameter. The fused kernel avoids the explicit repeat_interleave expansion on the GPU, saving memory during prefill.
CPU-only inference for LLaMA models with GQA. Supports quantised KV caches and sliding window for extended context even on consumer hardware.

GQA introduces no new security surface beyond standard transformer risks. Its primary reliability concern is incorrect repeat_interleave expansion: if the wrong axis is repeated or repeat is used instead of repeat_interleave, each query head silently attends to the wrong KV group — the outputs are numerically valid but semantically wrong, with no exception raised. Unit-test the attention output shapes and spot-check that queries in the same group produce similar attention patterns.

In multi-GPU tensor-parallel serving, each GPU shard must receive the correct slice of both Q heads and KV groups. For P GPUs: each GPU holds H/P query heads and G/P KV groups. Ensure G is divisible by both H and P; otherwise the KV sharding becomes uneven and requires padding.
GQA shares K/V projections across groups of query heads, reducing the KV cache by H/G while preserving near-MHA quality.
G = H is MHA; G = 1 is MQA. GQA at G = H/8 is the practical sweet spot balancing quality and efficiency.
The KV cache reduction directly increases achievable batch size during autoregressive decode, multiplying serving throughput without changing prefill speed.
Every production LLM above 7B released since 2023 — LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, Gemma, Falcon 40B — uses GQA by default.
Converting MHA to GQA requires mean-pooling KV weights within each group plus a short fine-tuning phase; simply pooling weights without retraining leaves a measurable quality gap.
In code, always use repeat_interleave (not repeat) when expanding G KV heads to H query heads; the two operations produce different head-to-group mappings.
HuggingFace Transformers, vLLM, and FlashAttention-2 all support GQA natively — prefer these implementations over custom code.
